item.title: “‘The destruction of the cord of continued existence, the destruction of the cord of continued existence,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is the…
item.description:
“‘The destruction of the cord of continued existence, the destruction of the cord of continued existence,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is the cord of continued existence? What is the destruction of the cord of continued existence?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, any mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies there are regarding form — that is called the cord of continued existence. Their destruction is the destruction of the cord of continued existence. Regarding feeling … perception … formations … Regarding consciousness, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, any mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies there are — that is called the cord of continued existence. Their destruction is the destruction of the cord of continued existence.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 23.3
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if a monk, reflecting thus, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with…
item.description:
“Monks, if a monk, reflecting thus, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with a mind overcome by sloth and torpor; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much unconcentrated’ — then that monk should make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. Just as, monks, if a turban or head were on fire, one would make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending to extinguish that turban or head — so too, that monk should make a strong wish … for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.51
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure: forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing; sounds cognizable…
item.description:
“Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure: forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing; sounds cognizable by the ear … odors … tastes … touches cognizable by the body — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Although they are not sensual pleasures, monks, in the noble one’s discipline they are called the strands of sensual pleasure.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.63
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object.…
item.description:
“So too, monks, for one who wanders outside his own proper range, into the domain of others, Māra gains an opportunity, Māra gains an object. And what, monks, is not a monk’s proper range, but the domain of others? It is the five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds … odors … tastes … touches cognizable by the body … desirable … enticing. This, monks, is not a monk’s proper range, but the domain of others. Monks, wander in your own proper range, your ancestral territory. When you wander in your own proper range, your ancestral territory, Māra does not gain an opportunity, does not gain an object. And what, monks, is a monk’s proper range, his ancestral territory? It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What four? Here, monks, a monk dwells contemplating the body as a body, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and sorrow in the world; dwells contemplating feelings as feelings … mind as mind … phenomena as phenomena, ardent, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and sorrow in the world. This, monks, is a monk’s own proper range, his ancestral territory.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 47.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, for one whose greed is not abandoned, whose hatred is not abandoned, whose delusion is not abandoned — he is called one who is…
item.description:
“Monks, for one whose greed is not abandoned, whose hatred is not abandoned, whose delusion is not abandoned — he is called one who is bound by Māra, trapped in Māra’s snare, subject to the Evil One’s will.

But, monks, for one whose greed is abandoned, whose hatred is abandoned, whose delusion is abandoned — he is called one not bound by Māra, freed from Māra’s snare, not subject to the Evil One’s will.

One who has abandoned greed, hatred, and delusion,
Him they call one with a developed mind,
One who has become Brahmā, the Tathāgata, the Buddha,
The one who has overcome enmity and fear,
The one who has abandoned all — such a one is called thus.”

From: Itivuttaka 68
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Rādha, whatever is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Rādha, is suffering? The eye, Rādha, is suffering — there you should…
item.description:
“Rādha, whatever is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. And what, Rādha, is suffering? The eye, Rādha, is suffering — there you should abandon your desire. Forms … Eye-consciousness … Eye-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on eye-contact … that feeling is suffering. There you should abandon your desire. The ear … The nose … The tongue … The body … The mind … Phenomena … Mind-consciousness … Mind-contact … Whatever feeling arises dependent on mind-contact … that feeling is suffering. There you should abandon your desire. Whatever is suffering, Rādha — there you should abandon your desire.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.77
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as lack of clear comprehension. For…
item.description:
“I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as lack of clear comprehension. For one lacking clear comprehension, monks, unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.68
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Form, monks, is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of form — that too is suffering. Form that has arisen from suffering, monks,…
item.description:
“Form, monks, is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of form — that too is suffering. Form that has arisen from suffering, monks, how could it be happiness? Feeling … Perception … Formations … Consciousness is suffering. Whatever cause and condition for the arising of consciousness — that too is suffering. Consciousness that has arisen from suffering, monks, how could it be happiness? Seeing thus, monks, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form … disenchanted with feeling … disenchanted with perception … disenchanted with formations … disenchanted with consciousness. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate … He knows: ‘… there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.19
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what one intends, what one plans, and what one has a tendency towards — that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When…
item.description:
“Monks, what one intends, what one plans, and what one has a tendency towards — that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When there is a basis, there is an establishment of consciousness. When consciousness is established and grows, there is the descent of name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense-bases; with the six sense-bases as condition, contact; with contact as condition, feeling … craving … clinging … existence … birth; aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. Such is the origin of this whole mass of suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.39
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, for an untaught ordinary person, contacted by feeling born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. From that craving, volitional formation arises. Thus, monks, that…
item.description:
“Monks, for an untaught ordinary person, contacted by feeling born of contact with ignorance, craving arises. From that craving, volitional formation arises. Thus, monks, that formation is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That craving is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That feeling is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That contact is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. That ignorance is impermanent, conditioned, dependently arisen. Monks, knowing thus, seeing thus, the defilements are immediately destroyed.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.81
📖 Read this sutta
